name: Sacred Cenotes of the Maya
longDescription:
Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico The cenotes (from Maya "dzonot") of the Yucatan Peninsula are natural sinkholes formed when limestone
                bedrock collapses, revealing underground pools connected to vast subterranean river systems. For the
                ancient Maya, these crystal-clear pools were far more than water sources - they were sacred portals
                to Xibalba, the watery underworld realm of the dead, and the dwelling places of Chaak, the rain god.
                The Maya believed cenotes were the mouths of the earth through which they could communicate with
                the gods, and many became sites of pilgrimage, ritual, and human sacrifice. The most famous, the
                Sacred Cenote at Chichen Itza, yielded hundreds of artifacts and human remains when dredged,
                testifying to centuries of offerings to the lords of the underworld.
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
For the
                ancient Maya, these crystal-clear pools were far more than water sources - they were sacred portals
                to Xibalba, the watery underworld realm of the dead, and the dwelling places of Chaak, the rain god. The Maya believed cenotes were the mouths of the earth through which they could communicate with
                the gods, and many became sites of pilgrimage, ritual, and human sacrifice.

geography: Often features distinctive natural formations associated with sacred narratives

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World and the Maize God
keyMyths.description: The Popol Vuh recounts how the Hero Twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque descended into Xibalba, the Maya underworld, to defeat the Lords of Death. Their victory ensured the rebirth of the Maize God and the continuation of life, linking cenotes to the origin of sustenance and cyclical renewal.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Chaac, the Rain God
keyMyths.description: Cenotes were crucial for accessing groundwater in the Yucatan Peninsula. The Maya believed that Chaac, the rain god, resided in these waters and controlled rainfall. Offerings, including jade, pottery, and even human sacrifices, were cast into cenotes to appease Chaac and ensure plentiful harvests.
keyMyths.source: Diego de Landa, Relación de las Cosas de Yucatán

keyMyths.title: The Entrance to Xibalba
keyMyths.description: Cenotes served as entrances to Xibalba, the Maya underworld ruled by the Lords of Death. Souls of the deceased were believed to journey through the cenotes to reach the afterlife, emphasizing the cenotes' connection to death, rebirth, and the cyclical nature of existence.
keyMyths.source: Various Maya codices and archaeological evidence

keyMyths.title: The Well of Sacrifice at Chichen Itza
keyMyths.description: The Sacred Cenote at Chichen Itza, also known as the Well of Sacrifice, was a major pilgrimage site. Archaeological excavations have revealed numerous offerings, including human remains, suggesting ritual sacrifices were performed to appease the gods and ensure the well-being of the community.
keyMyths.source: Alfred Tozzer, Comparative Study of the Mayas and the Lacandones

keyMyths.title: Guardians of the Cenotes: Aluxes
keyMyths.description: Aluxes, small mischievous spirits, were believed to inhabit and guard cenotes and other natural places. They could bring good fortune or misfortune depending on how they were treated, highlighting the importance of respecting the sacred nature of cenotes.
keyMyths.source: Modern Maya oral traditions and ethnographies

sources.name: Popol Vuh
sources.description: The creation myth and foundational text of the K'iche' Maya.
sources.title: Popol Vuh

sources.name: Diego de Landa, Relación de las Cosas de Yucatán
sources.description: A 16th-century account of Maya culture by a Spanish friar, providing insights into their beliefs and practices.
sources.title: Diego de Landa, Relación de las Cosas de Yucatán

sources.name: Alfred Tozzer, Comparative Study of the Mayas and the Lacandones
sources.description: An anthropological study examining Maya culture and rituals.
sources.title: Alfred Tozzer, Comparative Study of the Mayas and the Lacandones

sources.name: INAH (Instituto Nacional de Antropología e Historia)
sources.description: The National Institute of Anthropology and History of Mexico, which conducts archaeological research and publishes findings on Maya sites.
sources.title: INAH (Instituto Nacional de Antropología e Historia)

sources.name: Robert J. Sharer and Loa P. Traxler, The Ancient Maya
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Maya civilization, covering its history, culture, and religion.
sources.title: Robert J. Sharer and Loa P. Traxler, The Ancient Maya
